receipt_name: Three suspicious contacts in one afternoon
scenario: A person receives a bank text, a package-delivery SMS, and a boss-style gift-card email while trying to work.

contact_events.requested_action: Tap a link and enter login details to stop a suspicious transfer.
contact_events.pressure_tactic: Account-locked urgency and fear of losing money.
contact_events.risk_if_true: A real account issue could need prompt attention through the bank's official channel.
contact_events.risk_if_fake: Login details, one-time codes, or payment information could be stolen.
contact_events.source_of_truth: Known bank app, card-back phone number, or official website typed manually.
contact_events.notes: Do not use the SMS link or reply thread.

contact_events.requested_action: Pay a small redelivery fee through a shortened link.
contact_events.risk_if_true: A real delivery may need rescheduling through the carrier account.
contact_events.risk_if_fake: Card information and address details could be captured.
contact_events.source_of_truth: Carrier app, merchant order page, or tracking number typed into the official carrier site.
contact_events.notes: Check whether a package is actually expected.

contact_events.requested_action: Buy gift cards quickly and send photos of the codes.
contact_events.risk_if_true: A real manager request should still follow purchasing rules.
contact_events.risk_if_fake: Gift-card codes are effectively cash and usually cannot be recovered.
contact_events.source_of_truth: Known internal chat, direct call to the manager, or finance/purchasing policy.
contact_events.notes: Never send gift-card codes by email.

verification_route: Pause before responding; screenshot or save the message without clicking its links.
Use a known-good route: official app, bookmarked site, card-back number, internal directory, or in-person confirmation.
Check whether the requested action matches normal policy or past behavior.
If money, login, identity, codes, downloads, or secrecy are involved, escalate to the relevant owner before acting.
do_not_do: Do not click links in the suspicious message.
Do not share one-time codes, passwords, gift-card codes, identity numbers, or payment details.
Do not call phone numbers supplied only inside the suspicious message.
Do not let urgency substitute for a known source of truth.
safe_next_step: Verify the highest-risk message first through a known-good route, then block/report/delete only after preserving any evidence needed for the account owner or workplace process.
boundary_notes: Synthetic example only; do not paste real scam messages containing private information into public tools.
This is not legal, banking, cybersecurity, or identity-theft recovery advice.
Emergency threats, financial loss, account compromise, and identity exposure require official reporting routes.
